title: KL Divergence Loss
description:
Implement KL Divergence loss, used in VAEs and knowledge distillation.

KL divergence from Q to P:
```
KL(P || Q) = sum_i P_i * log(P_i / Q_i)
```

For a batch (N distributions, each over C classes):
```
L = (1/N) * sum_i KL(P_i || Q_i)
```

Gradient w.r.t. Q (the predicted distribution):
```
dKL/dQ_i = -P_i / Q_i / N
```

Clip Q to [eps, 1] to avoid log(0).

**Why this matters:** KL divergence is the ELBO term in VAEs (KL between posterior and prior N(0,I)). In knowledge distillation, it measures how far student logits are from teacher logits.
constraints: P is the target distribution (ground truth), Q is the predicted distribution
Return (kl_loss scalar, gradient w.r.t. Q of shape (N, C))
starter_code:
import numpy as np

def kl_divergence_loss(P: np.ndarray, Q: np.ndarray) -> tuple[float, np.ndarray]:
    """KL Divergence loss KL(P || Q) and gradient w.r.t. Q.
    
    Args:
        P: Target distributions, shape (N, C), rows sum to 1
        Q: Predicted distributions, shape (N, C), rows sum to 1
    
    Returns:
        Tuple of (kl_loss scalar, gradient w.r.t. Q of shape (N, C))
    """
    pass
solution_code:
import numpy as np

def kl_divergence_loss(P: np.ndarray, Q: np.ndarray) -> tuple[float, np.ndarray]:
    N = P.shape[0]
    Q_clip = np.clip(Q, 1e-8, 1.0)
    P_clip = np.clip(P, 1e-8, 1.0)  # Avoid log(0) in P too
    kl_per_sample = np.sum(P_clip * np.log(P_clip / Q_clip), axis=1)  # (N,)
    loss = float(np.mean(kl_per_sample))
    grad = -P_clip / Q_clip / N  # (N, C)
    return loss, grad
explanation:
**Key concepts:**

1. **Asymmetry**: KL(P||Q) != KL(Q||P). KL(P||Q) penalizes Q placing zero mass where P has mass (mass-covering behavior).

2. **Non-negative**: KL divergence is always >= 0, with equality iff P=Q (Gibbs' inequality).

3. **VAE usage**: KL(N(mu, sigma) || N(0,1)) = -0.5 * sum(1 + log(sigma^2) - mu^2 - sigma^2). This closed form is used in the reparameterization trick.

4. **Knowledge distillation**: Student loss = alpha*CE(hard_labels, student) + (1-alpha)*KL(teacher_soft || student_soft).

test_cases.name: Identical distributions give zero loss
test_cases.code:
P = np.array([[0.2, 0.5, 0.3]])
Q = np.array([[0.2, 0.5, 0.3]])
loss, _ = kl_divergence_loss(P, Q)
assert loss < 1e-6

test_cases.name: KL is non-negative
test_cases.code:
P = np.array([[0.3, 0.4, 0.3], [0.1, 0.7, 0.2]])
Q = np.random.dirichlet([1,1,1], 2)
loss, _ = kl_divergence_loss(P, Q)
assert loss >= 0.0

test_cases.name: Known KL value (2-class)
test_cases.code:
P = np.array([[0.5, 0.5]])
Q = np.array([[0.25, 0.75]])
loss, _ = kl_divergence_loss(P, Q)
expected = 0.5*np.log(0.5/0.25) + 0.5*np.log(0.5/0.75)
assert abs(loss - expected) < 1e-6

test_cases.name: Gradient shape is (N, C)
test_cases.code:
N, C = 5, 4
P = np.ones((N, C)) / C
Q = np.ones((N, C)) / C
loss, grad = kl_divergence_loss(P, Q)
assert grad.shape == (N, C)

test_cases.name: No NaN for clipped zero probabilities
test_cases.code:
P = np.array([[1.0, 0.0, 0.0]])
Q = np.array([[0.0, 0.5, 0.5]])
loss, grad = kl_divergence_loss(P, Q)
assert not np.isnan(loss) and not np.any(np.isnan(grad))

hints: Clip Q (and P) to avoid log(0)
Gradient of KL w.r.t. Q_i is -P_i / Q_i / N
